Brand Guidelines · v1.0

How we look, sound, and
show up as Polynize Agentic.

This is the internal brand document for Polynize.ai, the agentic arm of Polynize. It exists so that anyone building for us, writing for us, or making decisions about how we show up in the world can do so without having to guess.

Document
Unlisted · noindex
Audience
Team · Collaborators · AI agents
Status
Living document
Part One

Brand Foundation

Before we talk about colours and hooks, we need to be clear on what we actually are, who we're for, and why we exist right now. This section uses a simple five-stage journey, Understanding our world, the Challenge, Strategic Exploration, What Success Looks Like, and the Recommendation, to walk through the brand's foundation end to end. Everything downstream in this document, visual language, voice, content, claims, is an expression of these five answers.

01
Understanding our world

Who we are. Where we sit inside Polynize.

02
The Challenge

The real problem we are solving.

03
Strategic exploration

How we choose to solve it.

04
What success looks like

The destination we are taking clients to.

05
The recommendation

How we show up in the market.

1.4What success looks like

We don't measure success in tools deployed, tokens used, or AI "adoption rates." We measure it in the distance between where a client's business was before we arrived and where it is three months later.

For the client

  • They stop drowning in their day. Things that used to take hours happen in minutes.
  • They compete with companies 5–10× their size without having to hire 5–10× the headcount.
  • They stop context-switching between tools because the team lives inside the tools they already use.
  • They spend less time executing and more time thinking, deciding, and leading.
  • They feel in control of their AI, not confused by it.

For the market

Polynize Agentic becomes the company small and mid-sized business leaders come to when they're ready to build a team they couldn't otherwise afford. We become the proof that AI doesn't have to mean replacement, generic tools, or a loss of control, it can mean leverage, configured expertise, and a genuine unfair advantage for the people who were being priced out of traditional scale.

For us

We build a category, Agentic Teams for small and mid-sized businesses, and we own it. Our position is earned because our clients can point to real agents, doing real jobs, producing real results, named after real decisions their human lead made.

1.5The Recommendation, how we show up

Every decision in this document flows from this single recommendation: show up as the team that builds you a team.

That means:

  • Directness over hype. Real capabilities, real costs, real timelines. No "unlock the future of AI."
  • Specificity over abstraction. Named agents, named jobs, named tools, named outcomes.
  • Humans elevated, never erased. Every piece of content, every visual, every price comparison has to reinforce that the human is leading, we're not removing them.
  • Cognitive, not robotic. We avoid robot-hand, circuit-board, binary-code visual clichés. We're about thinking, not machinery.
  • Confidence through proof. We say specific things because we can back them. When we can't back a claim yet, we don't make it.
  • Warmth through founder voice. We sound like the founders we're selling to, because we are them.

The rest of this document is how to put that recommendation into practice across every surface where Polynize Agentic shows up: in a social post, in a deck, on the website, in a sales email, inside an agent's system prompt. If a decision ever feels unclear, come back to this section and re-read it. Everything downstream is an expression of these five answers.

Part Two

Who we're for

Every line of copy, every post, every visual choice in this brand is tuned for a specific kind of person. If the work doesn't speak clearly to this person, it's off-brand, no matter how well-designed it is. This section defines exactly who that person is, what they're living through right now, and why Polynize Agentic is built for them specifically.

2.1Our core audience: the overwhelmed SME

Our core demographic is small and mid-sized businesses: founders, operators, and leaders who are carrying too much work for the team they can currently afford. They feel the pressure of the markets and world around them, they know they need more capability, but they don't have the budget to build out the team they'd need through traditional hiring.

That gap, between the capability they need and the capability they can afford, is where we step in. Polynize Agentic gives them a team they couldn't otherwise have, configured with expert thinking, installed into the tools they already use.

Primary Persona
The SME founder or operator who's running at capacity

2–50 people, growing faster than their team can keep up with, carrying cognitive load they can't offload anywhere.

Who they are
  • Founder, operator, or department lead at an SME (typically 2–50 people)
  • Running a business that's working, but fragile under the weight of its own momentum
  • Wearing five hats, none of them well enough
  • Often technical enough to understand what AI is, not technical enough to build it themselves
  • Neurodivergent or neurodivergent-adjacent thinking is common, they see patterns, they spot opportunities, they just can't always execute on all of them at once
What they're living through
  • Drowning in operational work that shouldn't be theirs to do
  • Watching competitors with more resources pull ahead
  • Missing opportunities because they can't respond fast enough
  • Feeling like every tool they try is generic and every consultant is too expensive
  • Aware that AI could help but paralysed by the sheer number of options
What they secretly want
  • To stop being the bottleneck in their own business
  • To compete with companies much larger than them without the associated headcount cost
  • Leverage that actually feels like theirs, configured to their business, not a generic tool
  • To feel in control again, of their time, their work, and their trajectory
  • A team that works while they sleep
What stops them buying
  • Scepticism that AI can actually do the specific thing they need
  • Fear of spending money on something that won't land
  • Worry about losing control or being "replaced" by their own tools
  • Not knowing where to start or what a good build even looks like
  • Previous bad experiences with generic AI tools that promised everything

Sectors we see this show up in most

This persona isn't tied to a single industry. We see it consistently across:

  • Professional services: agencies, consultancies, accounting, legal, advisory firms
  • Creative and content businesses: media, production, content studios, brand shops
  • Investment and deal flow: angel syndicates, VC scouts, small funds, family offices
  • Property and real estate: agencies, buyer advocates, developers
  • Coaching, training, education: solo operators and boutique practices scaling knowledge
  • E-commerce and DTC brands: small teams running product, marketing, and ops at once
  • Construction and trades: owners juggling quoting, invoicing, client follow-ups, scheduling

2.2Moving up the stack

SMEs are where we start and where we're built for. But the shape of what we sell, configured Agentic Teams, human-led, with expert thinking installed, scales up the stack naturally. The language changes as the audience changes, even though the product is the same underneath.

Segment What we call it What it looks like in practice
SME
(our core)		 Your agent team 1–3 agents that replace the team they couldn't hire. Human lead is usually the founder.
Mid-market Agentic business units A coordinated team of agents deployed inside an existing department to extend its capacity. Human lead is the department head.
Enterprise Cognitive Operating Model deployment Multiple agent teams, a cognitive control layer, role redesign, pattern capture. This is where the full research framework (MHLL, Cognitive Unit, drift control) applies at organisational scale.

The important thing to remember: we don't chase enterprise by watering down our message for SMEs. The language gets more sophisticated as the audience does, but the message is the same: a human-led team of agents, with expert thinking installed, producing real outcomes without drift.

2.3Who we're not for

Saying who we're for only means something if we're also clear about who we're not for. Being specific here keeps us from wasting energy chasing the wrong leads and keeps our content sharp.

  • Pure tech enthusiasts shopping for the newest shiny AI thing. They want tools. We sell configured thinking. They'll always find a cheaper toy.
  • Businesses looking to fire their team. If a client's goal is replacement-first, we're the wrong partner. Our whole thesis is humans made more capable, not removed.
  • Companies that need a dashboard, not a team. We're not a SaaS subscription. We build teams that do work.
  • Organisations without a clear pain point. "We want to use more AI" is not a brief. "My sales follow-ups are falling through the cracks" is. If someone can't name the bottleneck, we help them find it, but they have to be willing to.
  • Anyone looking for the cheapest possible option. We're dramatically cheaper than hiring, but we're not free and we're not racing to the bottom.

2.4Signals we're talking to the right person

When a piece of our content lands with the right person, certain things happen. These are the signals we're on-target:

  • They say something like "finally, someone gets it", the scenario you described is the one they've been living.
  • They ask a very specific question about their specific situation. Specificity in the inbound is a green light.
  • They mention a role they've been meaning to hire but can't afford, and ask whether we can build an agent for it.
  • They want to see it working before they commit, and are willing to pay to design one once they do.
  • They want to keep control. They don't want to be replaced; they want to be amplified.

If your content is attracting leads that look like the opposite of this list, shoppers looking for the cheapest tool, people wanting to automate their staff out of jobs, or vague enterprise buyers who can't name a problem, the content is off-target. Re-read Part One.

Part Three

Visual Identity

The Polynize Agentic visual system is dark, precise, and confident. It avoids every AI cliché we can think of, no glowing blue brains, no robot hands, no rainbow gradients, no circuit boards. The whole point is to feel like a cognitive system: structured, deliberate, and calm, with sharp accents that call attention only to what matters.

3.1Colour system

Use these exact hex values. Do not substitute. The palette is intentionally small, most of the surface area is dark, with mint green used sparingly for emphasis and two supporting accent colours (electric blue and warm cream/gold) for specific roles.

Primary palette

Background
#0a0a0f
Card / Surface
#16161f
Text Primary
#f0f0f5
Text Secondary
#a0a0b0

Accent palette

Mint · Primary Accent
#69fccb
Electric Blue
#a5c1ec
Cream / Gold
#f0e1b6
Border
#2a2a3a

How each colour is used

  • Mint (#69fccb): our signature colour. Reserved for emphasis: primary CTAs, accent headlines, key metric numbers, icon strokes, dot indicators, small labels. If everything is mint, nothing is. Use sparingly.
  • Electric blue (#a5c1ec): the supporting accent. Used for sub-labels, supporting text inside cards, secondary data points, and subtle gradients that fade out of mint. Never the hero colour, always a supporting role.
  • Cream / gold (#f0e1b6): reserved almost exclusively for numbers, metrics, and data callouts. When we want a number to feel warm and valuable, a salary comparison, a throughput multiple, a cost saving, this is the colour.
  • Dark backgrounds: always. Never a white or light page. Sections alternate between pure dark (#0a0a0f) and the grid-textured card surface.
  • Borders (#2a2a3a): always 1px, always subtle. Borders are for structure, never for decoration.

Colour rules

  1. Dark background, always. No white.
  2. Only one hero accent colour per composition. If mint is doing the heavy lifting, gold and blue recede. If gold is the hero (like a pricing metric), mint and blue recede.
  3. Never mix mint and gold as decoration in the same element. Pick one.
  4. Gradients are subtle: mint at low opacity fading to transparent. Never rainbow, never heavy.
  5. Text is always either primary (#f0f0f5), secondary (#a0a0b0), or muted (#6a6a7a). No random greys.

3.2Typography

Two typefaces, used consistently across every surface.

  • Space Grotesk: headings, labels, numbers, anything structural. Used at weights 600–800, with tight negative letter-spacing on large sizes.
  • Inter: body text, UI copy, captions. Used at weight 400 for paragraphs, 500–600 for emphasis.
  • Caveat: used very rarely, only for small handwritten-style annotations like "real agent chat ↓". Not for body copy or headings.

Type scale

Space Grotesk
800 · 56px
-2px tracking
Display headline
Space Grotesk
700 · 32px
-1px tracking
Section headline H1
Space Grotesk
700 · 22px
Subsection heading H2
Space Grotesk
600 · 17px
Card heading / H3
Inter
400 · 15px
1.65 line
Body paragraph text, this is where we communicate ideas, descriptions, and content.
Inter
700 · 11px
2px tracking
Eyebrow label

3.3Logo and wordmark

The Polynize logo is a trefoil symbol in mint green paired with the wordmark set in Space Grotesk bold. For the agentic arm, the wordmark is paired with "POLYNIZE AGENTIC" when we need to explicitly brand as agentic, otherwise the parent Polynize wordmark is fine.

Do

  • Use the mint-on-dark variant as default, this is our primary surface.
  • Maintain clear space of at least the height of the wordmark on all sides.
  • Use the wordmark-only version at very small sizes where the symbol would lose legibility.

Don't

  • Don't recolour the logo outside the approved palette.
  • Don't distort, stretch, rotate, or outline the wordmark.
  • Don't place the logo on a busy photographic background.
  • Don't add drop shadows, glows, or other decorative effects.

3.4Iconography

  • Line icons only. 1.5px stroke. Rounded line caps and joins.
  • Stroke colour matches the accent role of the section, usually mint.
  • No filled illustrations, no 3D renders, no emoji-style icons.
  • Lucide or Heroicons outline style is our reference.
  • Icons sit inside rounded-corner tiles with a subtle mint glow background (rgba(105,252,203,0.08)) and a thin mint border (rgba(105,252,203,0.2)).

3.5Imagery direction

Our visual world is sculpted, geometric, and deliberately not-photographic. It's the visual language of thinking made visible.

Primary motif: low-poly geometric faces

Angular, faceted, almost sculpted human and agent faces. Always on dark backgrounds, often with subtle mint or blue rim lighting. These represent expert thinking made visible, the central idea of the brand.

Secondary motif: abstract thinking patterns

Subtle electricity, neural patterns, flowing lines connecting nodes. Dark and restrained. Used behind heroes and between sections to add texture without noise.

Always avoid

Do use
  • Low-poly / faceted face illustrations
  • Dark, moody photographic environments where agents are clearly integrated into real tools
  • Screenshots of Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack with agents visibly working inside them
  • Phone and laptop mockups with dark UI
  • Abstract node-and-line diagrams in mint or blue
Never use
  • Stock photos of people in offices smiling at laptops
  • Robot hands reaching out
  • Glowing blue brains or brain-with-circuit imagery
  • Binary code, rainbow gradients, circuit-board backgrounds
  • Humanoid robots or android faces
  • Handshake imagery, corporate meeting stock
  • AI-generated illustrations that look generic

3.6Layout and density principles

  • Breathing room over density. Default to more space, not less. Generous padding on cards. Long line-height on body text (1.65–1.7). Let content breathe.
  • Alternating section backgrounds. On long pages, alternate between pure dark and the grid-textured card surface to create rhythm. Never two sections in a row with the same background.
  • Max content width of ~1200px. Main content columns cap around 720–820px for readability.
  • Rounded corners. Cards use 12–20px radius. Buttons use 10–12px. Never perfectly sharp corners (except on full-bleed sections).
  • Subtle grid texture in background panels, ~48px grid at very low opacity. Adds structure without visual noise.
  • Icons, numbers, and eyebrow labels align left on cards unless a specific composition calls for centring.
Part Four

Voice & Language

The Polynize Agentic voice is the voice of a founder talking to another founder. Sharp, confident, warm, specific. It never sounds like a corporate AI vendor or a thought leader, and it never resorts to hedging or buzzwords. Everything in this section exists to make that voice repeatable, so anyone writing for us, including AI agents, can produce on-brand copy without second-guessing.

4.1Tone principles

Direct

We say what we mean. If a client would save $60K by building an agent instead of hiring a human, we say that. We don't hedge with "could potentially help you to reduce costs." We use real numbers, real timelines, and real names.

Confident, not arrogant

We back every bold claim with specifics. We're not afraid to say "25× cheaper" because we can show the maths. We're also honest about what we don't know yet and what's still being figured out. Confidence without evidence is noise.

Warm and human

Even though we're selling AI, we talk like a person, not a platform. First person is fine. Slight casual language is fine. Humour is fine when it lands. We're talking to another founder, not pitching from a stage.

Bold over safe

Given a choice between a polite phrase and a scroll-stopping one, we always pick the scroll-stopping one. "Your next hire doesn't need a salary." beats "Consider AI-powered solutions to augment your team." If the copy feels corporate, sharpen it.

Neurodivergent-native

Our content is written from a neurodivergent perspective and positioned as useful for anyone who recognises themselves in that thinking style. Short sentences. Visual hierarchy. Concrete over abstract. Real examples over theory. If a sentence can be cut in half without losing meaning, cut it in half.

Humans elevated, never erased

This is the most important principle and the easiest one to accidentally break. Every piece of copy has to leave the reader feeling more capable, not more replaceable. The human stays in the loop. We don't replace, we install. We don't remove, we amplify. If a sentence implies replacement, rewrite it.

4.2Our vocabulary

These are the words we use consistently across every surface. They're not jargon, they're the shared language of the category we're building. Use them the same way every time.

Core product
Agent

The unit we build. Not "bot", not "assistant" (except as a named role). Not "AI", not "tool".

Core product
Agentic Team

What we sell at scale. A coordinated team of agents working together under a human lead. Ties directly to the Agent Team Console.

Capability
Capability

One discrete thing an agent can do (e.g. "social media management"). Agents have a defined number of capabilities.

Integration
Connector

An integration to an external tool (Gmail, Telegram, Notion). Agents come with a set number of recommended connectors.

Deliverable
Blueprint

The build plan for an agent or agent team. What's produced from the Agent Builder experience.

Research
Cognitive Unit

The formal term (from Shourov's Cognitive Productivity paper). Used in longer-form content and B2B sales conversations.

Action verb
Deploy

What we do with agents. We don't "launch" them and we don't "release" them. We deploy them.

Action verb
Install

What we do with thinking. We install expert thinking into agents. We don't "program" or "configure", we install.

Hero concept
Expert thinking

The thing we actually sell. Every piece of copy should reinforce that what we install is expert thinking, not generic capability.

Problem language
Drift

The hidden cost of AI without structure. When outputs look confident but silently miss. The thing our configuration prevents.

Pricing language
Service fee

What we call the monthly. Not "hosting" and not "maintenance". It's a service fee, it reflects the ongoing service we provide.

Positioning
Human lead

The person who leads their agent team. We assume every agentic team has one, even if we don't always say it explicitly.

4.3Words we use vs. words we don't

Always use
  • Agent (not bot, not assistant, not AI)
  • Agentic Team (not "multi-agent system")
  • Install (not "configure", not "program")
  • Deploy (not "launch" or "release")
  • Service fee (not "hosting", not "monthly subscription")
  • Expert thinking (not "smart AI")
  • Trained on your business (not "customised")
  • Blueprint (for the build plan)
  • Human lead (for the operator)
  • Drift (for the hidden cost of bad AI)
  • Specific numbers, dates, and named tools
  • Named agents with personalities (Tabatha, Mia, etc.)
Never use
  • Unlock, leverage, revolutionize, game-changer, empower
  • Delve, cutting-edge, in today's fast-paced world
  • Robust, seamless, intuitive, powerful
  • Chatbot, AI assistant (generic form), bot
  • Replace, replacement, fire, eliminate headcount
  • Hosting, maintenance, monthly subscription
  • Solution, platform, ecosystem, synergy
  • Imagine if you could… / What if… / Are you struggling with…
  • Powered by AI, AI-driven, AI-powered
  • Thought-leader phrasing ("In a world where…")
  • Em dashes (house rule: we never use them. Use commas, periods, or colons instead)
  • Round, vague claims without numbers
  • Anything that implies the human is being removed

4.4How we talk about humans and agents

This is the most important linguistic rule in the brand. Get this one wrong and the whole positioning collapses.

We compare agents to human salaries because the market still thinks in those terms, and because the cost gap is the clearest, fastest way for a busy founder to understand the value on offer. A founder who's been quoted $85K for a social media manager needs to see the $2,500 build fee next to that number to feel the leverage viscerally.

But the comparison is for legibility, not replacement. Our actual position is that humans are more capable when they have an agent team working underneath them, the point is leverage, not substitution.

The right framing

"Building an agent for this role costs 25× less than hiring for it. That's not because we're replacing the human, it's because the real value is in the configuration. With a human lead in place, a well-built agent team delivers 2–5× throughput versus the equivalent hire. You get both: lower cost and higher leverage."

Acceptable

  • "Hire a human vs. build an agent", as a comparison of cost
  • "Your next hire doesn't need a salary", playful, still implies augmentation
  • "The role you couldn't afford to hire", acknowledges the real constraint
  • "25× cheaper than hiring", factual cost comparison, no replacement claim

Not acceptable

  • "Fire your [role]", replacement-coded
  • "Stop hiring. Start deploying.", replacement-coded, retired
  • "Replace your team with AI", against the core thesis
  • "Headcount reduction through AI", off-brand for us, even if technically true
  • Anything that frames the human as the problem
Part Five

Content System

This section exists so that anyone, a human writer, a social media AI agent, or a contractor, can sit down and produce on-brand content without having to guess. It covers the content pillars we return to, the formats that work on each platform, a library of approved hooks, and a reusable post structure. If you're writing something and it doesn't fit one of these patterns, it might be off-brand.

5.1Content pillars

Five themes we consistently return to. Every post should anchor on at least one of them. Over a month, the balance should be roughly even across the five, if one is dominating, the content is getting repetitive; if one is missing, the positioning is getting one-dimensional.

Pillar 01
The hidden cost of drowning

Named, specific pain points that SME founders are living through. Drowning in ops work. Missing opportunities. Losing to bigger competitors. The problem we solve, made visceral.

Pillar 02
Named agents, named jobs

"Meet Tabatha, the deal flow agent we built for X." First-person agent intros, personalities, specific capabilities. Makes the product tangible.

Pillar 03
The hiring maths

Cost comparisons that reframe how people think about capacity. "The sales rep you were going to hire for $90K… vs. the agent team that costs $2,500 to build and runs every day." Reinforces the value legibly.

Pillar 04
Humans, elevated

Stories and framings where the human is the hero. A founder reclaiming their week. A department lead who finally stopped being the bottleneck. The human lead is always visible in these posts.

Pillar 05
Behind the build

How we think about it. The concept of drift. Why configuration beats tooling. Short explainer content that positions us as builders with a point of view, not just vendors.

Pillar 06
The Polynize perspective

Our angle on the wider AI-and-work conversation. The cognitive unit. The real bottleneck. The shift from execution-heavy to cognitive operating models. Thought leadership without the thought-leader voice.

5.2Formats that work

Before/After hiring comparison

One of our most consistently effective formats. Pure cost legibility. "Hire a social media manager = $65K/year + recruitment + leave. Build one with Polynize = $2,500 one-off + service fee. Same work, 17× less cost." Works especially well on LinkedIn carousels, TikTok slideshows, and Instagram reels.

First-person agent introductions

Written as if the agent itself is talking. "I analyse incoming pitch decks, score deal quality using your investment criteria, and manage your pipeline so you never miss a high-potential opportunity." Puts the agent into the scene as a character, not a feature list.

Concrete scenario walkthroughs

Put the agent in a real moment, not in theory. "Monday 9:00am, Tabatha pings you on Telegram: 'Three invoices overdue, client X is 30 days late. Draft follow-up?'" Show the work happening in the tools people already use.

The cost reveal

Start with the human cost, pause, reveal the agent cost. Simple, repeatable format. Works as a hook in 15-second video or as a headline in a carousel.

The drift story

A cautionary post about what happens when AI gets deployed without configuration. Someone else's horror story. Our setup line is: "This is the hidden cost of AI without expert thinking installed." Positions us against both the hype and the tool-only vendors.

The day-in-the-life

Long-form format, usually YouTube or carousel. "A day with Tabatha the deal flow agent." Three scenarios showing proactive work, on-demand responses, and a weekly summary. Inspired by our Blueprint document structure.

5.3Hook library

Use these, adapt these, or write new ones in the same spirit. All hooks lead with specificity, concrete numbers, or a sharp reframe, never vague questions or "Imagine if…". All of them reinforce the "human-led agent team" positioning, not replacement.

Cost-reframe hooks

01The role you couldn't afford to hire, we just built it for $2,500.
02Your next hire doesn't need a salary.
03I built a deal flow agent in five days. It costs 20× less than the analyst I was about to hire.
04Built in a week. Works 24/7. Never asks for a raise.
05The $85K salary you were going to pay, reallocated, honestly.

Agentic team hooks

06You don't need a bigger team. You need a team that works while you think.
07One founder. Four agents. One business that finally scales.
08This is what a small business with a big team actually looks like now.
09Stop wearing five hats. Deploy five agents and wear the one that matters.
10Your agent team, ready to work, in the same tools you already live in.

Drift and configuration hooks

11Most AI looks impressive and quietly misses. That's called drift, and it's the real cost of AI without expert thinking installed.
12The problem isn't that AI is bad. The problem is that nobody installed a way of thinking into it.
13Cheap AI is easy. Configured AI is the unfair advantage.
14Every AI tool looks the same. Ours comes with an expert installed.

Leverage and throughput hooks

152–5× more output, from the same person you already are.
16One human. Three agents. The research says 2–5× more throughput. Our clients say it out loud.
17You're not trying to work harder. You're trying to stop being the bottleneck.
18The work isn't the problem. The doing-it-all-yourself is the problem.

Identity and reframe hooks

19You're not buying AI. You're buying an expert's way of thinking, installed.
20We don't build chatbots. We build cognitive teams.

5.4Post structure template

A reusable structure for a standard social post, short-form video script, or LinkedIn update. You can break it, but if you're stuck, this works.

  1. Hook: one line, bold, concrete, ideally with a number. Pulled or adapted from the hook library.
  2. Problem: one or two lines. Name the pain point specifically. Use the language your ICP actually uses.
  3. Solution: show the agent doing the thing. Ideally a named agent, a specific task, and a real tool (Telegram, Gmail, Slack).
  4. Proof: a number, a timeline, or an outcome. Cost saving, throughput, "built in a week".
  5. CTA: "Design your agent at polynize.ai/agents", "Book a call at polynize.ai/links", or "Link in bio".

5.5Platform notes

TikTok, @polynize.labs

  • Vertical 9:16. Captions on screen at all times.
  • First 2 seconds have to stop the scroll. Lead with the sharpest line from the hook library.
  • Keep under 45 seconds unless it's a full client story.
  • Music is low-key electronic, lo-fi, or silent with strong captions, avoid trending pop unless the joke depends on it.
  • The Polynize Agentic account on TikTok is @polynize.labs (NOT personal).

Instagram, @polynize.labs

  • This is the Polynize Agentic Instagram handle, not a personal account.
  • Reels are a first-class format. Treat them the same as TikTok, the same vertical, the same hooks, the same rhythm. Cross-post with platform-native text where possible.
  • Grid: dark backgrounds consistently, mint accents as the unifying element.
  • Carousels work especially well for "hire a human vs. build an agent" comparisons, and for "A Day With [Agent Name]" scenario walkthroughs.
  • Don't overload slides with text, let the palette and structure do the work.

LinkedIn, linkedin.com/in/marrscoiro

  • This is Marrs's personal profile, not a company page, for now.
  • Longer form is fine here. First-person founder voice.
  • Real client stories, real numbers, real lessons. No abstract thought-leader energy.
  • Most effective formats: the cost reframe, the drift story, and the Polynize Perspective pillar.
  • Post substance over frequency, we're builders, not pundits.

YouTube, @polynize-labs

  • Long-form builds, agent demos, "a day with this agent" walkthroughs, and founder interviews.
  • YouTube Shorts are a first-class format. Treat them the same as TikTok / Reels. Cross-post the same vertical content with YouTube-native captions.
  • Thumbnail rules: dark background, one big mint-green or gold number or word, one clear subject. No open-mouth thumbnail clichés.
  • Titles: specific and concrete. "I built a deal flow agent for $2,500" beats "How AI Is Changing Investing".
Part Six

Claims & Compliance

A short, strict section. These rules exist so we don't damage the brand by making claims we can't back, and so we stay out of trouble when we make promotional or comparative statements. When in doubt, the rule is: if we can't prove it with specifics, we don't say it.

6.1Claims we can make

  • "Up to 25× cheaper than hiring." This is backed by comparing our build cost to the midpoint salary of the equivalent role. We can show the maths for any role we list on the site.
  • "2–5× throughput." Backed by the research in Shourov's Cognitive Productivity paper on Cognitive Units in human–AI work. Always frame this as "a well-configured agent team", not as a blanket guarantee.
  • "Live in a week." This is our build cadence for a single-agent starter build. For larger team builds or custom capabilities, scope may extend, don't promise a week on a Team / Business Unit build without qualifying.
  • "Trained on your business." True, every build includes a private session where the client's context and thinking are installed into the agent.
  • Named client stories. Always gold. Use whenever we have permission. Specific > generic every time.

6.2Claims we can't make (yet)

  • "Thousands of businesses trust us." Not yet. Don't inflate adoption numbers.
  • "Guaranteed ROI in 30 days." We don't guarantee outcomes. We design for them, but we never promise them on a timeline.
  • "Replaces an entire department." Off-brand in every direction, both factually and against our "humans elevated" principle.
  • Anything comparing us to a named competitor by name unless it's clearly opinion, clearly backed, and clearly legal.
  • Professional-advice replacement claims. Never imply that an agent replaces a legal, medical, financial, or regulated professional without a human in the loop.

6.3Talking about pricing

Our pricing language has changed. Use these terms consistently.

  • Build fee: the one-off cost to design, train, and deploy an agent or agent team. Not "setup fee", not "installation fee".
  • Service fee: the monthly cost. Not "hosting and maintenance", not "monthly subscription". The monthly is a service fee, reflecting the ongoing service we provide alongside the agent team.

Current pricing is under review and is deliberately not hard-coded into this document. Always pull live numbers from the pricing page at polynize.ai/pricing when writing promotional content. If you're writing copy that references pricing and it's been more than a week since you checked the live page, check it again.

If the current pricing is in a promotional window, the promotional nature must be clear in any public copy, "promotional", "limited time", or a specific end date, so there's no ambiguity about what's being offered. Any monthly rate commitments (e.g. "locked in for X months") must be stated alongside the promotional price.

6.4Links we always include

  • Terms of Service: https://polynize.io/misc/tos.pdf. Link in the footer of every page and in promotional copy where claims are being made.
  • Agent Builder: polynize.ai/agents. The primary CTA destination for most content.
  • Book a call: the Calendly link (or wherever the current booking link lives). Secondary CTA for high-intent content.
  • Resources: polynize.ai/links. Use this as the "link in bio" destination on Instagram and TikTok so people land on a single hub.
Part Seven

Quick Reference

The one-screen version of everything above. If someone on the team only reads one section of this document, make it this one. It captures the non-negotiables and gives a quick decision framework for any on-brand call.

The one thing to remember

Polynize Agentic installs repeatable, world-class thinking into agent teams so small and mid-sized businesses can operate with the cognitive leverage of a much larger company, without needing the headcount to match.

Who we're for

SME founders and operators drowning in work, feeling market pressure, needing a team they can't afford to hire traditionally. Core is SMEs; Agentic Teams language lets us move up into mid-market and enterprise without changing the product.

What we are

The agentic arm of Polynize. We productise the Cognitive Unit (human + agent leverage + business fit) as named Agentic Teams with a human lead.

Non-negotiables

DimensionDoDon't
Colour Dark background, mint accent, gold for numbers White backgrounds, rainbow gradients, neon glows
Voice Direct, specific, warm, founder-to-founder Hedged, buzzwordy, thought-leader, corporate
Word for product Agent · Agentic Team Assistant · Bot · AI tool
Monthly pricing Service fee Hosting · Maintenance · Subscription
Human framing Elevated · Amplified · Human lead Replaced · Fired · Eliminated
Numbers Specific, backed, real Round, vague, unprovable
Hero visual Low-poly face, dark scene, real-tool screenshot Stock photo, robot hand, glowing brain
CTA "Design your agent" / "Book a call" "Learn more" / "Get started"

The decision question

If a sharp founder, drowning in work, saw this, would they scroll past it, or stop?

If the answer is scroll, it's not sharp enough. Come back to Part One and re-read the Recommendation.
